event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers settle the Isthmus of Panama, leaving fluted projectile points at sites such as Lake La Yeguada and the Madden Lake region.
Early communities begin cultivating maize, manioc, and other crops, marking the shift toward settled agricultural life on the isthmus.
Ceramic-producing villages emerge, with Monagrillo on the Pacific coast yielding some of the oldest pottery known in the Americas.
Chiefdom societies develop in central Panama, producing distinctive polychrome pottery and goldwork at sites like Sitio Conte.
The Coclé culture flourishes, renowned for elaborate gold ornaments, painted ceramics, and richly furnished elite burials at Sitio Conte.
Spanish explorer Rodrigo de Bastidas becomes the first European to sail along Panama's Caribbean coast.
Christopher Columbus, on his fourth voyage, explores the Caribbean coast and attempts to found a settlement at Belén.
Santa María la Antigua del Darién is founded, becoming one of the first permanent European settlements on the American mainland.
Vasco Núñez de Balboa crosses the isthmus and becomes the first European to sight the Pacific Ocean from the Americas, claiming it for Spain.
Pedro Arias Dávila founds Panama City on the Pacific coast, which becomes a key hub for Spanish exploration and trade.
The Audiencia of Panama is established, organizing Spanish colonial administration over the isthmus.
Welsh privateer Henry Morgan sacks and burns Panama City, forcing its relocation a few kilometers west to its present site.
Scotland launches the ill-fated Darién colonial scheme, which collapses within two years amid disease and Spanish hostility.
Panama is incorporated into the Viceroyalty of New Granada, shifting its administration toward Bogotá.
Panama declares independence from Spain and voluntarily joins Simón Bolívar's Gran Colombia.
Panama City hosts the Congress of Panama, Bolívar's pioneering effort to unite the newly independent Latin American republics.
Following the dissolution of Gran Colombia, Panama remains part of the Republic of New Granada, later Colombia.
The Mallarino-Bidlack Treaty grants the United States transit rights across the isthmus in exchange for guaranteeing Colombian sovereignty.
The Panama Railroad opens, becoming the first transcontinental railway and a major route for travelers during the California Gold Rush.
A French company led by Ferdinand de Lesseps begins construction of a sea-level canal across Panama.
The French canal effort collapses amid massive financial losses and tens of thousands of deaths from malaria and yellow fever.
Panama declares independence from Colombia with U.S. backing and signs the Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty granting the U.S. control of a Canal Zone.
The United States begins construction of the Panama Canal after taking over the French concession.
The Panama Canal opens to maritime traffic, transforming global trade and shipping routes.
The Guna people of the San Blas islands stage a revolt against forced assimilation, leading to recognition of their autonomy.
The nationalist Acción Comunal group overthrows the government in Panama's first coup d'état.
President Arnulfo Arias is ousted in a coup shortly after promulgating a new nationalist constitution.
Panama becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Anti-American riots over flag rights in the Canal Zone, known as Martyrs' Day, leave more than twenty Panamanians dead.
The National Guard, led by Omar Torrijos, seizes power in a coup, beginning years of military-dominated rule.
Omar Torrijos and U.S. President Jimmy Carter sign the Torrijos-Carter Treaties, agreeing to transfer the canal to Panama by 1999.
General Omar Torrijos dies in a plane crash, opening a power struggle within the military.
Manuel Noriega consolidates control over the Panama Defense Forces and becomes the country's de facto ruler.
The United States invades Panama in Operation Just Cause, ousting Manuel Noriega, who is later convicted on drug trafficking charges.
Panama abolishes its standing army through a constitutional amendment.
Panama assumes full control of the Panama Canal as the United States completes the handover, and Mireya Moscoso becomes its first female president.
Panama takes over administration of the former Canal Zone, integrating its lands and ports into the national economy.
Panamanians approve a referendum to expand the canal with a new, larger set of locks.
Panama City opens Central America's first metro system, easing chronic urban congestion.
The expanded Panama Canal opens, doubling capacity, while the Panama Papers leak exposes the country's offshore financial industry.
Mass protests and a Supreme Court ruling lead to the closure of the major Cobre Panamá copper mine over environmental and sovereignty concerns.
José Raúl Mulino wins the presidency, while a historic drought reduces Panama Canal water levels and limits ship transits.
